---
title: "Why Cocogitto for semantic release"
description: |
  Continuous delivery through using semantic releases reduces manual effort,
  time, and potential errors. This post describes why we use Cocogitto for the
  automated versioning and releasing of our software.
date: "2026-05-26"
categories:
  - communicate
  - organise
---

## Context and problem statement

We originally used [Commitizen](/why-semantic-release-with-commitizen/index.qmd)
for automating our versioning and releasing, but as our needs and projects
evolved, we encountered many limitations with it. For example:

- It sometimes would release something that based on Conventional Commit
  messages that shouldn't have triggered a release. Debugged didn't lead into
  any explanation on why this was happening.
- It didn't have a lot of configuration customisations that we needed for the
  diverse number of projects (e.g. websites, R packages) that we build and
  maintain.
- With increasing supply chain attacks on package registers like PyPI and npm,
  we wanted to use tools that were more secure and that had better security
  practices for these types of issues.
- Commitizen's GitHub Action workflow was quite complicated and difficult to
  assess the security risk of and to debug. This was actually one of the biggest
  issues we had with it (related to the point about about triggering releases
  that shouldn't have been triggered) and a big reason for needing to switch to
  something else. For instance, the workflow requires passing a GitHub secret
  token to a Docker container, without the ability to modify or improve on that
  practice.

So we needed to switch to something else.

## Decision drivers

::: content-hidden
List some reasons for why we need to make this decision and what things have
arisen that impact work.
:::

We already described a lot of available tooling in our [previous
post](/why-semantic-release-with-commitizen/index.qmd), so we won't cover those
again (unless they changed substantially). We need a tool that:

- Has a simple, easily reviewing GitHub Action workflow, or at the least a way
  that makes it easy to use in a workflow, e.g. as a CLI command run through
  `run`.
- Built by a more secure language, and installable without depending on another
  tool to install.
- Allows the use of GitHub's built-in `secrets` system in the workflows.
- Can be used as a CLI tool, so that if a good GitHub Action workflow isn't a
  barrier to using it.
- Has good configuration customisation for different types of projects.

## Considered options

::: content-hidden
List and describe some of the options, as well as some of the benefits and
drawbacks for each option.
:::

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/about/) keeps a
good list of tools that make use of their standard, which is where what we used
to find tools to compare and use. However, since the previous post, there is
really only one other option that fit our needs:

- [Cocogitto](https://docs.cocogitto.io/)

### Cocogitto

[Cocogitto](https://docs.cocogitto.io/) is a Rust CLI tool

::: columns
::::: column
#### Benefits

- Has very good customisations, including pre- and post-bump hooks that can run
  custom commands before and after the version is bumped.
- The GitHub Action workflow is very simple and doesn't require passing any
  secrets to it.
- It's built in Rust, which is a more secure language.
:::::

::::: column
#### Drawbacks

- Isn't as frequently updated, though Rust packages don't require as much
  maintenance as Python or JavaScript ones, so this might not be an issue.
- Custom pre- and post-bump hooks mean we have to write custom commands
  ourselves, which could be more work compared to using a tool that has more
  built-in functionality.
:::::
:::

## Decision outcome

::: content-hidden
What decision was made, use the form "We decided on CHOICE because of REASONS."
:::

We didn't have any choices here, as there was really only one tool that fit our
needs. But, Cocogitto is a well-developed tool that fits what we need.

### Consequences

::: content-hidden
List some potential consequences of this decision.
:::

- It isn't as frequently updated compared to other active tools, but we don't
  know yet if that will be a problem, as Rust packages don't tend to need as
  much maintenance as other languages require.
